items.html:
Welcome to Epilepsy Awareness

Welcome to this awareness course on epilepsy. Epilepsy is a common condition in which a person has a tendency to have seizures. A seizure happens when there is a sudden burst of intense electrical activity in the brain, which briefly disrupts how the brain normally works. This course is for care workers in England and is grounded in national guidance from NICE (NG217) and information from Epilepsy Action and the Epilepsy Society.

  • Awareness: your role is to support the person, give safe seizure first aid and recognise an emergency.
  • Individual: epilepsy affects every person differently, so you follow their care plan.
  • Knowledge only: this e-learning covers what you need to know, not hands-on competence.

This course covers the knowledge element of epilepsy awareness. Giving emergency or rescue medication, such as buccal midazolam, is a separate matter: it is only ever given by staff who have specific training, assessed competence and an individual protocol for that person. Nothing in this course qualifies you to administer it.

What this course covers

Over the next pages you will build a clear picture of epilepsy and your part in supporting someone who lives with it.

  • Understanding: what epilepsy is, how common it is and your role.
  • Seizures: the main types, and the auras and triggers that can precede them.
  • First aid and emergencies: what to do, when to call 999, and how to give ongoing support.

Throughout, keep the people you support in mind. Epilepsy is different for each person, so the detail that matters most will be written in their care plan. Your job is to know the general principles here and to combine them with what you are told about each individual you care for.

What is epilepsy?

Epilepsy is a condition that affects the brain and gives a person a tendency to have seizures. Our brains work by passing tiny electrical signals between millions of cells. In a seizure, a sudden, intense burst of this electrical activity disrupts the normal messages, and this is what causes the changes you might see or that the person feels.

  • Electrical activity: a seizure is a sudden burst of intense electrical activity in the brain.
  • A tendency: epilepsy usually means having a tendency to have seizures that start in the brain.
  • Not always known: for many people the cause of their epilepsy is never fully known.

One seizure on its own does not always mean a person has epilepsy. A diagnosis is usually made by a specialist doctor after more than one seizure, and only they can confirm it.

A varied condition

Epilepsy is not one single thing. What a seizure looks like depends on where in the brain the electrical activity happens and how far it spreads. Some seizures involve the whole brain from the start, while others begin in just one part. This is why two people with epilepsy can have very different experiences.

Many people with epilepsy have their seizures well controlled with anti-seizure medication and lead full, active lives. Others continue to have seizures despite treatment. Epilepsy can also sit alongside other conditions, and it is more common in people with a learning disability. Whatever the pattern, the person is an individual first, and epilepsy is only one part of who they are. Understanding this helps you offer support that fits the person, not a label.

items.question: What best describes what happens in the brain during a seizure?

answers.text: A sudden burst of intense electrical activity briefly disrupts how the brain normally works
answers.feedback: Correct. A seizure is caused by a sudden burst of intense electrical activity in the brain.

answers.text: The brain stops receiving any blood
answers.feedback: No. A seizure is caused by electrical activity in the brain, not by a loss of blood supply.

answers.text: A physical injury forms on the surface of the brain
answers.feedback: Not quite. Seizures are caused by bursts of electrical activity, not by an injury forming during the seizure.

answers.text: The person is simply pretending or seeking attention
answers.feedback: No. Seizures are real events caused by electrical activity in the brain and are never put on.

items.html:
Common, and your role

Epilepsy is one of the most common serious neurological conditions. In the United Kingdom many people live with it, across all ages and backgrounds, so it is likely you will support someone with epilepsy during your work. Knowing how to respond calmly and correctly is part of giving good, safe care.

  • Support: help the person live well and follow their care plan.
  • Safe first aid: keep the person safe during a seizure.
  • Recognise and escalate: spot an emergency and get help quickly.

You are not expected to diagnose or treat epilepsy. Your role is awareness: to support, to give safe seizure first aid, and to recognise when a seizure has become an emergency so you can escalate it.

Working alongside others

You are one part of a wider team around the person. That team may include their family, their GP, an epilepsy specialist nurse, a neurologist, and your own colleagues and manager. Good support depends on everyone sharing accurate information, and you are often the person who sees the individual day to day.

This means your observations matter. Reading and following the person's care plan, recording seizures accurately, and passing on any changes you notice all help the specialist team keep the person's care right. If you are ever unsure what to do, ask your manager rather than guess. Awareness is not only about the moments of a seizure; it is also about the steady, reliable support you give around them, and about knowing the limits of your own role.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about your role.
items.textField: Your role is one of awareness. You *support* the person, give safe seizure *first aid*, and *recognise* an emergency so that you can *escalate* it for help. You are not expected to diagnose or treat epilepsy.

items.html:
Different types of seizure

There are many types of seizure, and part of awareness is recognising the main ones. Seizures are often grouped by where they start in the brain. Some involve both sides of the brain from the beginning and are called generalised; two of the most widely known are tonic-clonic seizures and absence seizures.

  • Tonic-clonic: the convulsive seizure many people picture, with stiffening then jerking.
  • Absence: a brief blanking out, more common in children.
  • Recognition: knowing what you are seeing helps you respond correctly.

You do not need to name a seizure type perfectly. What matters is that you notice a seizure is happening, respond safely, and afterwards describe accurately what you saw.

Tonic-clonic and absence seizures

A tonic-clonic seizure is the type most people think of as a convulsion. The person usually loses consciousness. Their body first goes stiff (the tonic phase), often falling if they are standing, and then their muscles jerk rhythmically (the clonic phase). They may go pale or bluish around the lips, make noises, bite their tongue, or lose control of their bladder or bowels. Afterwards they are often confused, exhausted and in need of rest.

An absence seizure looks very different and can be easy to miss. The person briefly loses awareness, as though blanking out or daydreaming, for a few seconds. They may stop what they are doing, stare, and perhaps flutter their eyelids, then carry on with no memory of it. Absence seizures are more common in children. Because they are so brief and quiet, you may only notice them if you know to look.

items.question: Which description best fits a tonic-clonic (convulsive) seizure?

answers.text: The person loses consciousness, their body stiffens and then their muscles jerk rhythmically
answers.feedback: Correct. Stiffening followed by rhythmic jerking, with loss of consciousness, is typical of a tonic-clonic seizure.

answers.text: The person briefly stares and blanks out for a few seconds, then carries on
answers.feedback: Not quite. This describes an absence seizure rather than a convulsive tonic-clonic seizure.

answers.text: The person notices a strange smell but stays fully aware throughout
answers.feedback: No. That describes a focal aware seizure, not a convulsive tonic-clonic seizure.

answers.text: The person has a headache but no change in movement or awareness
answers.feedback: No. A tonic-clonic seizure involves loss of consciousness with stiffening and jerking.

items.html:
Focal seizures

Not all seizures involve the whole brain. A focal seizure (sometimes called a partial seizure) starts in just one part of the brain. What happens depends on which part is affected, so focal seizures can take many forms. An important difference between them is whether the person stays aware or not.

  • Focal aware: the person stays fully conscious and can remember it.
  • Focal impaired awareness: the person's awareness is affected during the seizure.
  • Varied: signs can be unusual sensations, movements or behaviour.

Focal seizures are not always obvious to an onlooker, so knowing that they exist helps you understand behaviour that might otherwise seem puzzling.

Aware and impaired awareness

In a focal aware seizure the person stays conscious and knows what is happening, even though they may not be able to control it. They might notice a strange taste or smell, a rising feeling in the stomach, tingling, twitching in one part of the body, or a sudden emotion such as fear. Because they remain aware, they can often tell you about it afterwards.

In a focal impaired awareness seizure the person's awareness is affected. They may seem confused or distant, and can behave in ways that look strange or aimless — plucking at their clothing, wandering, smacking their lips, mumbling, or repeating a movement. They are not in control of this and will usually not remember it. It is important not to mistake this for the person being difficult or drunk. Stay with them, keep them safe from hazards, and let the seizure run its course.

items.question: During a focal impaired awareness seizure, a person may wander or fumble with their clothing and not be aware of what they are doing.
items.correctMsg: Correct. In a focal impaired awareness seizure the person's awareness is affected, and they may behave in ways they cannot control or remember.
items.wrongMsg: This is true. In a focal impaired awareness seizure the person's awareness is affected, and they may behave in ways they cannot control or remember.

items.html:
Auras and warning signs

Some people get a warning that a seizure is coming. This warning is called an aura, and it is actually a focal aware seizure that may go on to become a larger one. An aura is different for each person, so it helps to know what a particular individual experiences.

  • Sensations: a strange smell, a taste, or a rising feeling in the stomach.
  • Feelings: sudden fear, dizziness, or a sense of déjà vu.
  • Personal: the aura is often the same each time for that person.

If a person tells you they can feel a seizure coming, take it seriously. Helping them to a safe place and staying with them can prevent injury.

Common triggers

For some people, certain things make a seizure more likely. These are called triggers, and they vary from person to person. Knowing someone's triggers, where they have them, can help reduce how often seizures happen. Common triggers include:

  • Missed medication: not taking anti-seizure medication as prescribed.
  • Lack of sleep: being very tired or having a disturbed routine.
  • Stress and illness: emotional stress, or being unwell with a fever.

Other triggers can include alcohol and missed meals, and, for a small number of people, flashing or flickering lights — known as photosensitive epilepsy. It is worth remembering that flashing lights only trigger seizures in a minority of people with epilepsy, so this is not a risk for everyone.

items.question: Which of these is a common seizure trigger for many people with epilepsy?

answers.text: Missing doses of prescribed anti-seizure medication
answers.feedback: Correct. Missed medication is one of the most common triggers, which is why taking it as prescribed matters so much.

answers.text: Drinking plenty of water through the day
answers.feedback: No. Staying hydrated is not a seizure trigger; missed medication, lack of sleep and stress are common triggers.

answers.text: Going for a gentle daily walk
answers.feedback: Not quite. Gentle activity is not a trigger; common triggers include missed medication, lack of sleep and illness.

answers.text: Eating regular, balanced meals
answers.feedback: No. Regular meals help; it is missed meals, missed medication and lack of sleep that can act as triggers.

items.html:
First aid for a convulsive seizure

When someone has a tonic-clonic (convulsive) seizure, calm and simple first aid keeps them safe until it passes. You cannot stop the seizure, and you do not need to — your job is to protect the person from injury and stay with them. Remember the key actions:

  • Protect from injury: guide them gently and move dangerous objects away.
  • Cushion the head: put something soft under their head.
  • Time it: note when the seizure starts and how long it lasts.

Try to stay calm. Most seizures stop on their own within a few minutes, and your steady presence helps everyone around you stay calm too.

Protecting the person

As soon as you realise someone is having a convulsive seizure, look at the space around them. If they are on the ground, cushion their head with something soft such as a folded cushion or jumper to protect it from the hard floor. Remove harmful objects from nearby, or, if the person cannot be moved, move the hazards away — hot drinks, furniture with sharp corners, or anything they could knock into.

Do not try to move the person unless they are in a dangerous place, such as a road or by a fire. Loosen anything tight around their neck if you can do so easily, and note the time the seizure began. Speak gently and reassuringly, even if they cannot respond — hearing a calm voice can be comforting. Stay with them throughout, and keep other people back so the person has space and privacy.

items.question: A person you support falls and begins a convulsive seizure on the floor. What should you do first?

answers.text: Cushion their head, move nearby objects that could harm them, and note the time
answers.feedback: Correct. Protecting the head, clearing hazards and timing the seizure are the right first actions.

answers.text: Hold their arms and legs still to stop the jerking
answers.feedback: No. You must never restrain someone during a seizure; protect them from injury instead.

answers.text: Put a spoon or your fingers into their mouth
answers.feedback: No. You must never put anything in the person's mouth; cushion their head and clear hazards instead.

answers.text: Lift them onto a chair straight away
answers.feedback: Not quite. Do not move the person unless they are in danger; cushion their head and clear hazards where they are.

items.html:
What not to do

Knowing what not to do during a seizure is just as important as knowing what to do. Some old ideas about seizure first aid are wrong and can cause harm. Two rules matter above all:

  • Do not restrain: never hold the person down or try to stop their movements.
  • Nothing in the mouth: never put anything, including your fingers, in their mouth.

A person cannot swallow their tongue during a seizure. Putting objects or fingers in the mouth can break teeth, injure them or you, and block their airway. Let the seizure happen without trying to hold the person still.

The recovery position

Once the jerking movements have stopped, gently roll the person onto their side into the recovery position. This helps keep their airway clear and lets any saliva drain from their mouth. Tilt their head back a little so they can breathe easily, and stay with them while they come round.

After a seizure a person is often confused, drowsy, embarrassed or upset, and may not know where they are. Reassure them calmly, tell them they have had a seizure and that they are safe, and give them time and quiet to recover. Do not offer food or drink until they are fully alert. Check for any injuries, and respect their dignity — if they have been incontinent, help them discreetly once it is safe to do so.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the seizure first aid guidance.
items.textField: During a convulsive seizure, never *restrain* the person and never put anything in their *mouth*. Instead, *cushion* their head and *time* how long it lasts. Once the movements stop, place them in the *recovery* position.

items.html:
Timing and staying with the person

Timing a seizure is one of the most useful things you can do, because how long a seizure lasts helps decide whether it is an emergency. Note the time it starts and the time the convulsive movements stop. If you are not sure, it is safer to assume more time has passed and act accordingly.

  • Start the clock: check a watch or phone as soon as the seizure begins.
  • Stay with them: never leave the person alone during a seizure.
  • Watch and remember: note what you see so you can describe it later.

Staying calm and staying present are at the heart of good seizure first aid. Even when there is nothing more to do, your presence keeps the person safe.

Reassurance and dignity

A seizure can be frightening and undignified, both for the person and for anyone watching. Part of your role is to protect the person's dignity throughout. Keep onlookers back and give the person privacy, and afterwards speak gently and without fuss. Many people feel embarrassed or upset after a seizure, especially if it happened in front of others or if they lost control of their bladder or bowels.

Reassure the person that they are safe and that you are there to help. Do not bombard them with questions while they are still confused; simply let them know what has happened and stay close until they feel ready. Treating someone with warmth and respect after a seizure matters as much as the practical first aid. It tells the person that a seizure does not change how you see them, and it helps them keep their confidence.

When a seizure is an emergency

Most seizures stop by themselves and do not need an ambulance. But some are medical emergencies, and knowing the difference is a vital part of awareness. Call 999 for an ambulance if any of the following happen:

  • Lasting too long: a convulsive seizure lasts more than 5 minutes.
  • One after another: one seizure follows another without the person recovering in between.
  • Other danger signs: a first-ever seizure, a serious injury, or difficulty breathing.

If you are ever in doubt about whether to call 999, make the call. It is always better to seek help than to wait when someone may be seriously unwell.

Status epilepticus

A convulsive seizure that lasts longer than 5 minutes, or repeated seizures without recovery in between, is a dangerous situation called status epilepticus. The longer a convulsive seizure goes on, the more serious it becomes, which is why timing matters so much and why 5 minutes is the point to act.

You should also call 999 if a seizure happens in water, if the person is injured, is having trouble breathing, or does not regain consciousness after the jerking stops. Call too if it is the person's first-ever seizure, or if you simply do not know their history and have no care plan to guide you. Some people have a care plan that gives different instructions, for example if they usually have longer seizures — but if there is no such plan, follow the 5-minute rule and get help.

items.question: You are timing a convulsive seizure and it has now lasted more than 5 minutes. What should you do?

answers.text: Call 999 for an ambulance, as this may be a medical emergency called status epilepticus
answers.feedback: Correct. A convulsive seizure lasting more than 5 minutes needs an emergency ambulance.

answers.text: Wait another 15 minutes before doing anything
answers.feedback: No. A convulsive seizure lasting more than 5 minutes is an emergency; you should call 999 now.

answers.text: Put the person in the recovery position and leave them alone
answers.feedback: Not quite. You must call 999 for a seizure this long, and you should never leave the person alone.

answers.text: Give the person a drink to help them recover
answers.feedback: No. Never give food or drink during a seizure; a convulsive seizure over 5 minutes needs a 999 call.

items.html:
Rescue medication

Some people who are at risk of long or repeated seizures are prescribed rescue medication (also called emergency medication), such as buccal midazolam, which is given into the side of the mouth. It is used to stop a prolonged seizure before it becomes more dangerous, following the person's individual protocol.

  • Prescribed individually: it is prescribed for a named person, with their own protocol.
  • Specific training: only staff who are trained and assessed as competent may give it.
  • Not this course: awareness training does not qualify you to administer it.

If you have not had this specific training for that individual, you do not give rescue medication. Your role is to give first aid, call for help, and stay with the person.

Care plans and protocols

Everyone you support who has epilepsy should have information about their condition in their care plan. This may include what their seizures usually look like, how long they normally last, their known triggers, what to do, and when to call for help. Where rescue medication is prescribed, there will be an individual protocol setting out exactly when and how trained staff give it.

Reading and following the care plan is essential, because epilepsy is so individual. A plan might tell you that a particular person often has seizures lasting a few minutes, so that only longer ones need an ambulance. Without a plan, or if you are unsure, you fall back on the general rules in this course — including calling 999 for a convulsive seizure lasting more than 5 minutes. Always check the plan, and ask your manager if anything is unclear.

items.question: Any care worker who has completed epilepsy awareness training is allowed to give buccal midazolam rescue medication.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Rescue medication is only given by staff with specific training, assessed competence and an individual protocol — awareness training alone is not enough.
items.wrongMsg: This is false. Rescue medication such as buccal midazolam is only given by staff with specific training, assessed competence and an individual protocol; awareness training alone does not qualify you.

items.html:
Recording seizures

Accurate records of a person's seizures are one of the most valuable things you can provide. A seizure diary or record helps the person and their specialist team understand how the epilepsy is behaving and whether treatment needs to change. Because you are often there when a seizure happens, what you record really matters.

  • What you saw: what happened before, during and after the seizure.
  • How long: the time it started and how long it lasted.
  • Recovery: how the person was afterwards, and any injuries.

Record the facts clearly and promptly, following your workplace's system, and pass the information on so that it reaches the people who need it.

SUDEP — a sensitive subject

Very rarely, a person with epilepsy can die suddenly and unexpectedly, and no other cause is found. This is known as SUDEP, which stands for Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy. It is uncommon, and it is a distressing subject, but as someone who supports people with epilepsy it is right that you are aware of it.

The risk of SUDEP is thought to be lower when seizures are better controlled, which is one reason why taking medication as prescribed, recording seizures accurately, and getting help when needed all matter. You are not expected to counsel anyone about SUDEP; that is a conversation for the person, their family and their specialist team. What awareness means for you is taking seizures seriously, supporting the person to manage their epilepsy well, and reporting concerns so the specialist team can act. If the subject worries you, or someone you support, speak to your manager.

Person-centred, dignified support

Living with epilepsy is about far more than seizures. Many people have their epilepsy well controlled and want to live full, independent lives. Your support should help them do exactly that, treating them as an individual and never letting the condition define them.

  • Independence: support the person to do what they can and want to do.
  • Dignity: handle seizures and their aftermath with sensitivity and respect.
  • Accurate recording: keep clear, factual records that help their care.

Listen to the person, follow their care plan, and involve them in decisions about their own life. Good epilepsy support is person-centred first and foremost.

Bringing it together

Well done for completing this awareness course on epilepsy. You have learned that epilepsy is a common condition in which a sudden burst of electrical activity in the brain causes seizures; the main types of seizure and the auras and triggers linked to them; safe first aid for a convulsive seizure; and how to recognise an emergency, escalate it, and give ongoing, dignified support.

Remember the key point from the start: this course covers awareness and knowledge only. It helps you support people and give safe seizure first aid, but it does not qualify you to give rescue or emergency medication such as buccal midazolam — that is only ever given by staff with specific training, assessed competence and an individual protocol. Follow each person's care plan, record what you see accurately, and ask your manager whenever you are unsure. Carry this awareness into your everyday care & support.

items.question: What is the best summary of your role in supporting someone with epilepsy?

answers.text: Support the person, give safe seizure first aid, recognise emergencies and escalate, and record accurately
answers.feedback: Correct. Awareness means supporting, giving safe first aid, escalating emergencies and keeping good records.

answers.text: Diagnose the type of epilepsy and adjust their medication
answers.feedback: No. Diagnosis and medication are for the specialist team; your role is awareness and safe support.

answers.text: Give rescue medication whenever a seizure happens
answers.feedback: No. Rescue medication is only given by staff with specific training, competence and an individual protocol.

answers.text: Restrain the person to keep them still until the seizure stops
answers.feedback: No. You must never restrain someone during a seizure; protect them from injury and stay with them.
